title: Safeguarding Honest Speech Act
plain_summary: This bill stops the federal government from spending tax money to make government workers use special pronouns or chosen names that are different from someone's birth sex or legal name. If a government worker is told they have to use these special pronouns or names, they can file a complaint against their workplace. Workers can also sue the government and ask for money to pay for any harm caused and their lawyer fees. The bill is meant to protect government workers from being forced to use certain types of speech at work.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Safeguarding Honest Speech Act.

sections.title: Section 2 No Federal Funds for Compelled Language
sections.text:
No matter what any other law says, the federal government cannot use tax money to create or enforce rules that require federal workers or contractors to use specific language when talking about other people. Specifically, these workers cannot be forced to use pronouns that do not match a person's biological sex at birth. They also cannot be forced to use a name that is not the person's official legal name when talking about that person.

If a federal worker or contractor believes their agency is breaking this rule, they can write a complaint. The agency must respond in writing within thirty days. If the worker is not happy with the response, they can take the agency to court. If they win in court, they might get money to pay for damages and their lawyer fees. They might also get up to one hundred thousand dollars in punishment money. The court can also order the agency to stop breaking the rule. Workers must file their lawsuit within one year of when the rule breaking happened.

In this law, person means an individual human being. Sex means the biological sex a person was born with based on their body parts and genes at birth.
